metadata.subject: Social Studies
metadata.description: Social Studies 9 explores the political, social, economic, and technological revolutions that reshaped the world between 1750 and 1919. Students examine imperialism, migration, nationalism, global conflicts, and discriminatory policies. The course investigates how collective identities are constructed, how power disparities reshape relationships between groups, and how environmental and ideological factors influence change.
metadata.general_description: A study of world history from 1750 to 1919, tracing revolutions in politics, industry, and thought, and examining how imperialism, migration, and emerging nationalism transformed global society.

big_ideas.statement: Emerging ideas and ideologies profoundly influence societies and events.
big_ideas.elaboration: The Enlightenment, nationalism, liberalism, and socialism all reshaped political structures, sparked revolutions, and inspired independence movements around the world.

big_ideas.statement: The physical environment influences the nature of political, social, and economic change.
big_ideas.elaboration: Access to resources, geographic barriers, and climate shaped where industrialization took root, how empires expanded, and why certain nations became dominant powers.

big_ideas.statement: Disparities in power alter the balance of relationships between individuals and between societies.
big_ideas.elaboration: Imperialism created unequal relationships between colonizing nations and colonized peoples, with lasting economic, cultural, and political consequences.

big_ideas.statement: Collective identity is constructed and can change over time.
big_ideas.elaboration: Nations, ethnic groups, classes, and communities developed shared identities through shared history, culture, language, and resistance to external forces.

categories.name: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills
categories.raw: Use Social Studies inquiry processes and skills to ask questions; gather, interpret, and analyze ideas; and communicate findings and decisions.

competencies.raw: Ask questions and gather, interpret, and analyze ideas using Social Studies inquiry processes.
i_can_statements: I can develop my own inquiry questions about a historical event or issue.
I can collect information from multiple sources and evaluate which are most useful.
I can organize my findings clearly and communicate my conclusions in an appropriate format.
I can revise my thinking when I encounter new or conflicting evidence.

categories.name: Significance
categories.raw: Assess the significance of people, places, events, or developments, and compare varying perspectives on their historical significance.

competencies.raw: Assess historical importance and compare varying perspectives on historical significance.
i_can_statements: I can apply criteria to determine why a person, event, or idea is historically significant.
I can explain how significance looks different depending on whose perspective you take.
I can compare how the same event is remembered differently by different groups.

categories.name: Evidence
categories.raw: Assess the justification for competing historical accounts by examining source reliability, points of contention, and adequacy of evidence.

competencies.raw: Assess the justification for competing historical accounts by examining source reliability.
i_can_statements: I can compare two different accounts of the same event and explain why they differ.
I can evaluate whether a source is reliable based on its origin, purpose, and context.
I can identify what evidence is missing from a historical account.
I can construct a reasoned argument using historical evidence.

categories.name: Continuity and Change
categories.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups at the same time period.

competencies.raw: Compare and contrast continuities and changes for different groups at the same time period.
i_can_statements: I can explain how two different groups experienced the same historical era differently.
I can identify what changed and what stayed the same for a group across a historical period.
I can evaluate whether a change represented progress, decline, or both — depending on who you ask.

categories.name: Cause and Consequence
categories.raw: Evaluate how prevailing conditions and the actions of individuals or groups affect events, decisions, or developments.

competencies.raw: Evaluate how prevailing conditions and the actions of individuals or groups affect events.
i_can_statements: I can identify multiple causes of a historical event, distinguishing between background conditions and immediate triggers.
I can evaluate the role individuals played versus broader social forces in causing change.
I can trace both intended and unintended consequences of a decision or event.

categories.name: Perspective
categories.raw: Explain differing viewpoints and make reasoned ethical judgments about actions in the past and present.

competencies.raw: Explain different perspectives on past or present people, places, issues, or events by considering prevailing norms, values, worldviews, and beliefs.
i_can_statements: I can take on the perspective of someone from a different time, culture, or social group.
I can explain how someone's worldview shapes how they interpret an event.
I can avoid projecting modern values unfairly onto people from the past.

categories.name: Ethical Judgment
categories.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about actions in the past and present, and assess appropriate ways to remember and respond.

competencies.raw: Make reasoned ethical judgments about controversial actions in the past or present.
i_can_statements: I can make an ethical judgment about a historical action while considering the context of the time.
I can discuss whether people today have a responsibility to respond to historical injustices.
I can distinguish between condemning a past action and condemning the people who committed it.

areas.name: Revolutions
areas.raw: Political, social, and economic revolutions including the American, French, Industrial, and Haitian revolutions.
topics: The American Revolution: Enlightenment ideas and colonial independence
The French Revolution: causes, phases, Reign of Terror, and legacy
The Haitian Revolution: the only successful slave revolt leading to independence
The Industrial Revolution: causes in Britain, spread to Europe and North America
Social impacts of industrialization: urbanization, child labour, working conditions
Economic systems: capitalism, early socialism, and labour movements

areas.name: Imperialism and Colonialism
areas.raw: Imperialism, colonialism, and their impacts on colonized and colonizing nations.
topics: Motivations for imperialism (economic, political, ideological, religious)
The 'Scramble for Africa' and the Berlin Conference
British, French, and other European empires in Asia, Africa, and the Americas
Impacts on colonized peoples: economic exploitation, cultural suppression, displacement
Resistance movements against colonial rule

areas.name: Migration and Demography
areas.raw: Global migration patterns and demographic shifts during this period.
topics: Migration to the Americas: voluntary and forced (slavery, indentured labour)
Immigration to Canada: waves of settlement and push/pull factors
Internal migration due to industrialization and urbanization
The Irish Famine and emigration patterns
Demographic consequences of empire

areas.name: Nation-State Development
areas.raw: Development of nation-states including Canada, and the role of nationalism.
topics: Nationalism as an ideology and its connection to identity
Confederation and the formation of Canada (1867)
Unification of Germany and Italy
The decline of empires: Ottoman, Austro-Hungarian, Russian
First Peoples and the formation of Canada: treaties, resistance, and the Indian Act

areas.name: Regional and Global Conflicts
areas.raw: Regional and global conflicts of the era, including World War I.
topics: Causes of World War I: nationalism, imperialism, militarism, and alliances
Course and key battles of WWI
Canada's role in WWI and the conscription crisis
The war's human cost: casualties, trauma, and social change
The Treaty of Versailles and its consequences

areas.name: Discriminatory Policies and Injustices
areas.raw: Discriminatory policies and social injustices of the era.
topics: The transatlantic slave trade and abolitionism
Racial discrimination in colonial societies
The Chinese head tax and exclusion acts in Canada
The treatment of First Peoples in Canada: residential schools, land dispossession
Discrimination against women and the suffrage movement

areas.name: Canada's Physical Geography
areas.raw: Canada's physiographic features and natural resources.
topics: Canada's physiographic regions: Canadian Shield, Interior Plains, Cordillera, etc.
Natural resource distribution and its influence on settlement patterns
The role of geography in Canada's economic development
Environmental impacts of early resource industries
